danger_signals.meaning: Player scratch or consecutive missed shots result in loss of life; visual feedback of game-over approach

danger_signals.visual_form: Cue ball returns without pocket; turn control transfers
danger_signals.meaning: Indicates missed objective and loss of turn advantage in competitive modes

danger_signals.visual_form: Life loss in 9-Ball mode upon illegal shot
danger_signals.meaning: Violation of pool rules; immediate penalty state

safety_signals.visual_form: Flashing star appears in specific pocket during play
safety_signals.meaning: Opportunity for bonus points, extra lives, or bonus round; positive world state

safety_signals.visual_form: Ball enters pocket; points awarded on HUD
safety_signals.meaning: Progress toward level objective; safe state advancement
Player maintains control and momentum; reduced danger of life loss

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enhanced remakes feature five 'city' themed levels (Los Angeles, San Francisco, Las Vegas, New York City, Atlantic City) as visual/contextual progression markers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increasing number of balls on table per level; escalating score requirements; difficulty curve visualized through expanded rack complexity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lives system active in single-player Pocket Game; lives removed in two-player competitive modes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turn-based control switching in multiplayer; continuous penalty structure in single-player

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dedicated visual layout for 19 trick shot levels with increasing difficulty visual indicators
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Single-shot constraint; all-balls-pocketed-in-one-shot requirement; high-risk/high-reward point structure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lives counter reaches zero; HUD state change
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game loop reset; return to level start or game start

environmental_storytelling.example: Genesis/SNES versions use photo-realistic city backgrounds (Los Angeles, San Francisco, Las Vegas, NYC, Atlantic City) to contextualize billiards within American gambling/entertainment geography; visual setting communicates narrative of rising stakes and social context of competitive pool

Photo-realistic player character representation in Genesis/SNES/Game Gear versions designed as homage to 1986 film 'The Color of Money' (resembling Tom Cruise and Paul Newman characters); visual character design establishes cultural/cinematic context and aspirational narrative
Four different rack configurations become standard visual variation; communicates game complexity escalation and pool ruleset authenticity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Player controls trajectory, spin (english), special shots (massé, jump shots); visual feedback through aiming reticle
Visual despawn upon pocket; point addition to HUD; numerical order achievement triggers bonus state
Flashing visual animation in designated pocket; conditional bonus trigger if ball pocketed in same shot
Visual/behavioral handoff of input control between players upon missed shot or foul; turn indicator shifts

notes: Side Pocket is a pure mechanics-focused sports simulation with minimal narrative/political world-state elements. Visual communication centers on game status (lives, points, level progression) and rule-state feedback (valid/invalid shots, bonus activation). Faction and danger/safety signals are implicit to billiards rule structure rather than explicit game design. City-themed levels in enhanced versions (Genesis/SNES) provide minimal environmental narrative context—primarily cosmetic progression markers. The game's 'world-state' is entirely game-state (score, lives, level, mode); no dynamic faction systems, political conditions, or environmental storytelling beyond surface-level venue theming. Manifestation layer reflects purely ludic communication: HUD feedback, ball physics, turn control, and visual reward triggers.
